title: Assertions and Expectations
description: Master Playwright's powerful assertion library with auto-waiting capabilities to create reliable test verifications.
objectives: Learn different assertion types for various scenarios
Master element state and visibility checks
Handle soft assertions and custom error messages

sections.title: Understanding Assertions
sections.content:
# Playwright Assertions

Playwright's assertions are special - they **auto-retry** until the condition is met or timeout occurs. This eliminates flaky tests caused by timing issues.

## Auto-Retrying Behavior

When you write:
```typescript
await expect(page.locator('.status')).toHaveText('Success');
```

Playwright will:
1. Check if text is 'Success'
2. If not, wait 50ms and check again
3. Repeat until timeout (5 seconds default)
4. Pass if condition met, fail if timeout

This means your tests automatically handle dynamic content and async operations\!

## Two Types of Assertions

**Web-First Assertions** (auto-retry):
- `expect(locator).toBeVisible()`
- `expect(page).toHaveTitle()`
- Always use with `await`

**Generic Assertions** (no retry):
- `expect(value).toBe(expected)`
- `expect(array).toHaveLength(3)`
- Regular Jest assertions

Best Practices

keyTakeaways: Playwright assertions auto-retry until timeout, eliminating timing issues
Always use 'await' with web-first assertions like toBeVisible(), toHaveText()
Use negative assertions with .not for checking element absence
Soft assertions (expect.soft) continue test execution after failures
Custom timeouts can be set for specific assertions that need more/less time
Web-first assertions are preferred over manual value checks
Multiple specific assertions provide better debugging information than single generic ones
expect.poll() allows custom polling logic for complex conditions
